request.question: This U.S. state's oldest school, Berwick Academy, overlooks the Salmon Falls River which forms part of the state border with New Hampshire. Name the state.

title: Newichawannock Canal
Vaughan Woods State Park
Portland & Rochester Railroad
Salmon Falls River
Great East Lake
Lovell Lake
Berwick Academy (Maine)
Salmon Falls Mill Historic District
Little River (Salmon Falls River)
Horn Pond
sentences: The Newichawannock Canal is a man-made canal which drains Great East Lake into Horn Pond at the border between Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, in the northeastern United States.
It is at the head of the Salmon Falls River, which the Abenaki called "Newichawannock", meaning "river with many falls".

Begun in 1850 by the Great Falls Company to increase the water available for its mills in Somersworth, New Hampshire, the 0.75 mi canal is unusual as it was built for strictly industrial purposes in an area remote from the actual industrial site.
The canal forms the boundary between the states of New Hampshire and Maine, and is spanned by a stone bridge built at the same time.
The canal and bridge, along with related artifacts, were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2014.
Vaughan Woods State Park is a public recreation area located along the Salmon Falls River on the western edge of South Berwick, Maine, at the New Hampshire border.
The state park includes the restored Hamilton House, stands of old-growth forest, and 3 mi of hiking trails.
The lands were bequeathed to the state by Elizabeth Vaughan in 1949.
The Portland and Rochester Railroad, established in 1867, was an important predecessor railway of the Rochester to Portland branch line of the Boston and Maine Railroad.

It was founded in the merger of several smaller shortline rail transport companies, the oldest being the shortline York and Cumberland Railroad which was formed in 1846 to connect the seaport facilities of Portland, Maine, to the water powered manufacturing and textile industries along the Quampheagan Falls on the Salmon Falls River in the twin towns of South Berwick, Maine, and Rollinsford, New Hampshire (ca 1850).
The Salmon Falls River is a tributary of the Piscataqua River in the U.S. states of Maine and New Hampshire.

It rises at Great East Lake, Newichawannock Canal, and Horn Pond and flows south-southeast for approximately 38 mi , forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire.
Great East Lake is an 1825 acre water body located in Carroll County, New Hampshire, and York County, Maine, in the United States.
The lake is shared by the towns of Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, with 45% of its surface area in Acton and 55% in Wakefield.
The lake is the farthest upstream of the five headwater lakes of the Salmon Falls River, a tributary of the Piscataqua River.
Water from Great East Lake flows out its dam and through the 800 ft Newichawannock Canal into Horn Pond, then into the Salmon Falls River below Horn Pond.
Lovell Lake is a 538 acre water body in Carroll County in eastern New Hampshire, United States, in the town of Wakefield.
The lake is both spring-fed and fed by small streams including Horse Brook.
The outlet of the lake, located at the village of Sanbornville, is the beginning of the Branch River.
From here, the Branch River flows through Milton where it joins the Salmon Falls River on the Maine-New Hampshire border.
The Salmon Falls River empties into the tidal waters of the Piscataqua River northwest of Portsmouth, New Hampshire, then into the Gulf of Maine.
Berwick Academy is a college preparatory school located in South Berwick, Maine.
Founded in 1791, it is the oldest educational institution in Maine and one of the oldest private schools in North America.
The school sits on a 72-acre, 11-building campus on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire.

Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.
The Salmon Falls Mill Historic District encompasses a historic mill complex on Front Street in Rollinsford, New Hampshire.
The complex includes four major structures and seven smaller ones, on about 14 acre of land along the Salmon Falls River.

They were built between about 1840 and the mid-1860s, and have an unusual architectural unity, for additions made to the buildings were done with attention to matching elements of the existing structures.
The Number 2 Mill, built in 1848, was an early location where a turbine was used instead of a waterwheel to provide power to the mill machinery.
The district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.
The Little River is a 21.3 mi tributary of the Salmon Falls River in the U.S. state of Maine.
It rises from streams in York County, flows southwest through Lebanon, and reaches its confluence with the Salmon Falls River in Berwick.
Horn Pond is a 227 acre water body located on the border of Acton, Maine, and Wakefield, New Hampshire, in the United States.
The lake is both spring-fed and fed by the outflows of Great East Lake to the north and Wilson Lake to the east.

Horn Pond's outlet is the Salmon Falls River which forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire and empties into the Piscataqua River east of Dover, New Hampshire, and ultimately the Gulf of Maine.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: This U.S. state's oldest school, Berwick Academy, overlooks the Salmon Falls River which forms part of the state border with New Hampshire. Name the state.
hop1_docs: Berwick Academy (Maine) | Berwick Academy is a college preparatory school located in South Berwick, Maine.  Founded in 1791, it is the oldest educational institution in Maine and one of the oldest private schools in North America.  The school sits on a 72-acre, 11-building campus on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire.  Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.

Salmon Falls River | The Salmon Falls River is a tributary of the Piscataqua River in the U.S. states of Maine and New Hampshire.  It rises at Great East Lake, Newichawannock Canal, and Horn Pond and flows south-southeast for approximately 38 mi , forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire.

Vaughan Woods State Park | Vaughan Woods State Park is a public recreation area located along the Salmon Falls River on the western edge of South Berwick, Maine, at the New Hampshire border.  The state park includes the restored Hamilton House, stands of old-growth forest, and 3 mi of hiking trails.  The lands were bequeathed to the state by Elizabeth Vaughan in 1949.

Horn Pond | Horn Pond is a 227 acre water body located on the border of Acton, Maine, and Wakefield, New Hampshire, in the United States.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by the outflows of Great East Lake to the north and Wilson Lake to the east.  Horn Pond's outlet is the Salmon Falls River which forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire and empties into the Piscataqua River east of Dover, New Hampshire, and ultimately the Gulf of Maine.

Newichawannock Canal | The Newichawannock Canal is a man-made canal which drains Great East Lake into Horn Pond at the border between Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, in the northeastern United States.  It is at the head of the Salmon Falls River, which the Abenaki called "Newichawannock", meaning "river with many falls".  Begun in 1850 by the Great Falls Company to increase the water available for its mills in Somersworth, New Hampshire, the 0.75 mi canal is unusual as it was built for strictly industrial purposes in an area remote from the actual industrial site.  The canal forms the boundary between the states of New Hampshire and Maine, and is spanned by a stone bridge built at the same time.  The canal and bridge, along with related artifacts, were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2014.
Berwick, Maine | Berwick is a town in York County, Maine, United States, situated in the southern part of the state beside the Salmon Falls River.

Lovell Lake | Lovell Lake is a 538 acre water body in Carroll County in eastern New Hampshire, United States, in the town of Wakefield.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by small streams including Horse Brook.  The outlet of the lake, located at the village of Sanbornville, is the beginning of the Branch River.  From here, the Branch River flows through Milton where it joins the Salmon Falls River on the Maine-New Hampshire border.  The Salmon Falls River empties into the tidal waters of the Piscataqua River northwest of Portsmouth, New Hampshire, then into the Gulf of Maine.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Berwick Academy — [1] «Berwick Academy (Maine) |»: "it is the oldest educational institution in Maine"
- Berwick Academy location — [1] «Berwick Academy (Maine) |»: "overlooking the Salmon Falls River"
- Salmon Falls River border — [2] «Salmon Falls River |»: "forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire."
- Salmon Falls River natural border — [4] «Horn Pond |»: "forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire"

Ambiguities:
- None: "Berwick Academy" is identified as "Berwick Academy (Maine)"; "Salmon Falls River" is consistently associated with Maine and New Hampshire.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts: the passages explicitly state Berwick Academy is the oldest educational institution in Maine, and the river forms the Maine–New Hampshire border. Final answer can be given.

Retrieval direction:
- (for verification) fetch page "Berwick Academy (Maine)"
- fetch page "Salmon Falls River"
- fetch page "South Berwick, Maine"
Final answer: Maine.
hop2_docs: South Berwick, Maine | South Berwick is a town in York County, Maine, United States.  The population was 7,220 at the 2010 census.  South Berwick is home to Berwick Academy, a private, co-educational university-preparatory day school founded in 1791.

Berwick Academy (Maine) | Berwick Academy is a college preparatory school located in South Berwick, Maine.  Founded in 1791, it is the oldest educational institution in Maine and one of the oldest private schools in North America.  The school sits on a 72-acre, 11-building campus on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire.  Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.

Berwick Academy, Berwick-upon-Tweed | Berwick Academy (formerly Berwick Community High School) is a coeducational upper school and sixth form located in Berwick-upon-Tweed in the English county of Northumberland.
North Berwick, Maine | North Berwick is a town in York County, Maine, United States.  The town was set off from Berwick in 1831, following South Berwick in 1814.

Noble High School (Maine) | Noble High School is a public high school in North Berwick, Maine serving students in grades 8-12 from the towns of Berwick, North Berwick, and Lebanon.  NOBLE is an acronym for the three towns that NHS serves (NOrth Berwick, Berwick, and LEbanon).  Noble High School is a member of the Coalition of Essential Schools.

Berwick (CDP), Maine | Berwick is a census-designated place (CDP) in the town of Berwick in York County, Maine, United States.  The population was 2,187 at the 2010 census, out of a total of 7,246 in the entire town.  It is part of the Portland–South Portland–Biddeford, Maine Metropolitan Statistical Area.

North Berwick (CDP), Maine | North Berwick is a census-designated place (CDP) consisting of the primary settlement in the town of North Berwick in York County, Maine, United States.  The population was 1,615 at the 2010 census, out of a total town population of 4,576.  It is part of the Portland–South Portland–Biddeford, Maine Metropolitan Statistical Area.
